teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** An entire agreement clause (EAC) is a contractual provision declaring that the written document constitutes the whole agreement between the parties, superseding all prior negotiations, representations, and agreements. Its purpose is to exclude pre-contractual statements from becoming terms of the contract, thereby promoting contractual certainty.

**Leading Authority:** In *Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd* [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611, Lightman J upheld an entire agreement clause which stated that 'this agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties.' The clause was held to be effective to exclude a collateral warranty from becoming a term of the contract. A party seeking to rely on a pre-contractual statement must either show it was expressly incorporated into the written agreement or establish one of the recognised exceptions.

**Exceptions to Entire Agreement Clauses:** (1) **Misrepresentation** — an EAC cannot exclude liability for fraudulent misrepresentation, and s.3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 subjects clauses that would exclude liability for non-fraudulent misrepresentation to the requirement of reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (*AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd* [2011] EWCA Civ 133). (2) **Collateral Contracts** — if a separate collateral contract can be established on the basis of a pre-contractual statement, an EAC in the main contract may not prevent its enforcement. (3) **Rectification** — the court retains its equitable jurisdiction to rectify a written contract that does not reflect the parties' true common intention, notwithstanding an EAC. (4) **Implied Terms** — terms implied by statute or common law are not automatically excluded by a general EAC; express words and compliance with statutory controls are required.

**In short:** Entire agreement clauses are powerful but not absolute. They yield to fraud, are subject to reasonableness controls for misrepresentation liability, and do not automatically exclude collateral contracts or implied terms.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question: In __________ Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611, Lightman J held that an entire agreement clause was effective to exclude a collateral warranty from becoming a term of the contract unless the warranty was expressly stated in the written agreement.
feedback: Correct. *Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd* [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611 — the leading modern authority on the effectiveness of entire agreement clauses. Lightman J confirmed that a properly drafted EAC will prevent pre-contractual statements from being incorporated as contractual terms.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611

question: Match each exception to the entire agreement clause with its correct legal description:
answer: Misrepresentation=EAC cannot exclude liability for fraud; exclusion of non-fraudulent misrepresentation liability subject to s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 reasonableness test; Collateral Contract=A separate contract founded on a pre-contractual statement, which may be enforced despite an EAC in the main contract; Rectification=The court's equitable jurisdiction to correct a written contract that does not reflect the parties' true common intention; Implied Terms=Terms imported by statute or common law that may survive a general EAC unless expressly excluded in compliance with statutory controls

options.text: EAC cannot exclude liability for fraud; exclusion of non-fraudulent misrepresentation liability subject to s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 reasonableness test
A separate contract founded on a pre-contractual statement, which may be enforced despite an EAC in the main contract
The court's equitable jurisdiction to correct a written contract that does not reflect the parties' true common intention
Terms imported by statute or common law that may survive a general EAC unless expressly excluded in compliance with statutory controls

feedback: Well matched. These four exceptions demonstrate the limits of entire agreement clauses. An EAC is a powerful commercial tool, but it cannot oust the court's equitable jurisdiction (rectification), override mandatory statutory protections (misrepresentation, implied terms), or prevent a separate collateral contract from being enforced.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3

question: SoftSys Ltd enters a written contract with FinanceCo to provide IT services. The contract includes an entire agreement clause: 'This agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties and supersedes all prior representations, warranties and undertakings.' During pre-contractual negotiations, SoftSys's managing director told FinanceCo orally that the software was 'fully GDPR-compliant with no known vulnerabilities.' The software is found to be non-compliant and contains critical security flaws. FinanceCo wishes to sue. Applying *Inntrepreneur* [2000] and *AXA Sun Life* [2011], what is the correct legal analysis?

options.text: FinanceCo cannot claim at all because the entire agreement clause conclusively excludes all pre-contractual statements
options.feedback: Incorrect. An EAC does not exclude liability for misrepresentation, particularly where the clause purports to exclude liability for non-fraudulent misrepresentation — it is subject to the reasonableness test under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 and UCTA 1977.

options.text: FinanceCo can claim for breach of contract because the oral statement became an express term of the contract despite the EAC
options.feedback: Incorrect. Under *Inntrepreneur* [2000], the whole purpose of an EAC is to prevent such oral statements from becoming terms — the written agreement is the sole source of contractual obligations.

options.text: FinanceCo cannot claim for breach of contract because the EAC prevents the oral statement from becoming a term, but it may have a claim for misrepresentation under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 subject to the reasonableness requirement
options.feedback: Correct. Following *AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd* [2011] EWCA Civ 133, an EAC does not bar a misrepresentation claim, but any clause purporting to exclude liability for misrepresentation must satisfy the reasonableness requirement under UCTA 1977. The EAC prevents the statement from being a contractual term, but misrepresentation is a separate cause of action sounding in tort.

options.text: The entire agreement clause is void as an unlawful attempt to exclude liability for misrepresentation
options.feedback: Incorrect. EACs are not void per se. Under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967, the clause is subject to a reasonableness assessment under UCTA 1977, but that does not render it automatically void.

feedback: This scenario highlights the fundamental distinction between a claim for breach of contract (where a term has been broken) and a claim for misrepresentation (where a false statement induced entry into the contract). The EAC prevents the statement from becoming a term, so there is no breach of contract claim. But the misrepresentation claim survives the EAC — subject to the reasonableness test under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 and UCTA 1977. *AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd* [2011] EWCA Civ 133 is the key authority on this interaction.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'An entire agreement clause provides complete and absolute protection. If a written contract contains an EAC, no party can bring any claim whatsoever based on anything said or done before the contract was signed. The clause operates as an impenetrable shield, preventing claims for misrepresentation, collateral contracts, rectification, and implied terms. Courts are bound to give effect to EACs without exception because the parties have freely agreed to them.'
answer: An EAC does not provide absolute protection. It cannot exclude liability for fraudulent misrepresentation; exclusion of non-fraudulent misrepresentation liability is subject to reasonableness under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 and UCTA 1977. Collateral contracts, rectification, and implied terms are recognised exceptions that may survive an EAC.
feedback: Found it. An EAC is a powerful tool but subject to key exceptions: (1) it cannot exclude liability for fraud; (2) liability for non-fraudulent misrepresentation can only be excluded if reasonable under UCTA 1977 (*AXA Sun Life* [2011]); (3) it does not prevent enforcement of a separate collateral contract; (4) it does not oust the court's rectification jurisdiction; (5) it does not automatically exclude terms implied by statute or common law. An EAC is a contractual term, not an all-purpose immunity.
oscoaReferences: AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611

question: Which TWO of the following are correct statements about entire agreement clauses under English law?

options.text: An EAC conclusively excludes all claims based on pre-contractual statements, including those founded on fraud
options.feedback: Incorrect. Fraudulent misrepresentation cannot be excluded by contract. As a matter of public policy, no clause can exempt a party from liability for its own fraud.

options.text: An EAC is effective to prevent pre-contractual statements from being incorporated as terms of the written contract, as confirmed in *Inntrepreneur* [2000]
options.feedback: Correct. This is the core purpose of an EAC. *Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd* [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611 — the EAC successfully prevented a collateral warranty from becoming a term.

options.text: An EAC automatically excludes all implied statutory terms without the need for any additional wording
options.feedback: Incorrect. Implied statutory terms are not excluded by a general EAC alone. Express wording and compliance with statutory controls (e.g. UCTA 1977, CRA 2015) are required to exclude implied terms.

options.text: A misrepresentation claim may survive an EAC, but any term purporting to exclude liability for non-fraudulent misrepresentation must satisfy the reasonableness requirement under UCTA 1977
options.feedback: Correct. *AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd* [2011] EWCA Civ 133 — s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 subjects clauses excluding misrepresentation liability to the UCTA reasonableness test. The EAC does not kill the misrepresentation claim; it potentially limits it, subject to reasonableness.

options.text: EACs have no legal effect and are treated as mere statements of intent by the courts
options.feedback: Incorrect. EACs have significant legal effect. *Inntrepreneur* [2000] confirms they are substantive contractual provisions that prevent pre-contractual statements from attaining contractual status. They are not 'mere puffs.'

feedback: The correct answers are (b) and (d). An EAC effectively prevents pre-contractual statements from becoming terms (*Inntrepreneur* [2000]), but it does not bar misrepresentation claims — though any exclusion of misrepresentation liability must pass the UCTA reasonableness test (*AXA Sun Life* [2011]). Fraud can never be excluded.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question: BuildCo and LandCo enter a written construction contract with an entire agreement clause: 'This agreement embodies the entire understanding of the parties and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings whether oral or written.' Before signing, LandCo's director told BuildCo's director that the site had 'no archaeological restrictions.' After work begins, Roman remains are discovered, halting construction for 18 months. The contract is silent on delay risk. BuildCo sues LandCo. What is the correct analysis under *Inntrepreneur* [2000] and *AXA Sun Life* [2011]?

options.text: BuildCo has a claim for breach of contract because the pre-contractual statement about archaeology is an express term
options.feedback: Incorrect. The EAC prevents the pre-contractual statement from becoming a term. The written agreement is the sole source of contractual obligations under *Inntrepreneur* [2000].

options.text: BuildCo cannot claim for breach of contract because the EAC excludes the statement from being a term, but may claim for misrepresentation, subject to the EAC satisfying the s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 reasonableness test
options.feedback: Correct. The EAC prevents the 'no archaeological restrictions' statement from being a contractual term, so no breach of contract arises. However, the statement may constitute an actionable misrepresentation. Under s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967, to the extent the EAC purports to exclude liability for that misrepresentation, it must satisfy the UCTA 1977 reasonableness requirement — assessed at the time the contract was made.

options.text: BuildCo has no remedy whatsoever because the EAC is absolute
options.feedback: Incorrect. A misrepresentation claim is not automatically extinguished by an EAC. *AXA Sun Life* [2011] makes clear that misrepresentation claims require separate analysis under s.3 of the 1967 Act.

options.text: BuildCo can claim for breach of a collateral contract because LandCo's statement about archaeology was a separate promise
options.feedback: Incorrect. On these facts, the statement is more properly analysed as a representation rather than a separate contractual promise. BuildCo did not show additional consideration beyond entering the main contract, and LandCo's statement lacks the hallmarks of a collateral warranty — it was a statement of existing fact, not a promise as to the future.

feedback: This consolidates the key learning from Block A. The EAC successfully excludes pre-contractual statements from becoming terms (*Inntrepreneur* [2000]), but it does not provide carte blanche for misrepresentations. A separate misrepresentation claim may exist, and the court will apply the s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 reasonableness test to any clause that would exclude it. The question is always: was the clause reasonable at the time the contract was made?
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question: Under section ____ of the Misrepresentation Act 1967, any contractual term that would exclude or restrict liability for pre-contractual misrepresentations is subject to the requirement of reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.
feedback: Correct. Section 3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 (as amended) provides that a term excluding or restricting liability for misrepresentation is of no effect unless it satisfies the requirement of reasonableness as set out in s.11 of the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977. This is a crucial statutory control on entire agreement clauses that go beyond declaring the written terms and purport to exclude misrepresentation liability. The burden of proving reasonableness lies on the party seeking to rely on the clause.
oscoaReferences: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.11

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** A collateral contract is a separate contract that exists alongside and independent of the main contract between the parties. A pre-contractual statement, although not incorporated as a term of the main written contract — particularly where an entire agreement clause is present — may nevertheless form the basis of a binding collateral contract if the requisite elements are established.

**Requirements:** The classic formulation comes from *Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton* [1913] AC 30 (HL), where Lord Moulton stated that three elements must be proven: (1) the maker of the statement must have intended it to be relied upon as a contractual warranty; (2) the recipient must in fact have relied upon the statement; and (3) there must be consideration. Entering into the main contract can serve as consideration for the collateral promise.

**Leading Authority:** In *City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd* [1959] Ch 129, the landlord's agent told the tenant before the lease was signed that, despite a covenant prohibiting residential use, the tenant could continue to sleep on the premises. Harman J held that this oral assurance constituted a collateral contract which overrode the inconsistent written term. The tenant's entry into the lease provided consideration.

**Commercial Context:** Collateral contracts are a powerful mechanism for giving legal effect to pre-contractual assurances, especially where the main contract contains an entire agreement clause. In *J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd* [1976] 1 WLR 1078, an oral assurance — that goods would be shipped below deck — was held to create a collateral contract that overrode printed exclusion clauses. The case demonstrates the courts' willingness to uphold collateral promises in a commercial setting.

**In short:** A collateral contract can override even clear written terms. The three requirements — intention, reliance, and consideration — must be established on the evidence. But where they are, the written contract is not the last word.
oscoaReferences: City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30
J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078

question: In City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v __________ [1959] Ch 129, Harman J held that an oral assurance to a tenant that he could continue sleeping on the premises created a collateral contract that overrode an inconsistent written covenant prohibiting residential use.
feedback: Correct. *City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd* [1959] Ch 129 is the seminal authority on collateral contracts overriding written terms. Harman J held that the landlord's oral assurance induced the tenant to enter the lease — the entry into the lease was consideration for the collateral promise, and equity would not permit the landlord to resile from it.
oscoaReferences: City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129

question: Match each requirement for establishing a collateral contract with its correct legal description:
answer: Intention=The maker of the statement must intend it to be relied upon as a contractual warranty, not as a mere representation or opinion; Reliance=The recipient must in fact have relied on the statement when deciding to enter the main contract; Consideration=Entering into the main contract can constitute consideration for the collateral promise; Inconsistency=The fact that the collateral term contradicts a written term of the main contract is not necessarily fatal to the collateral contract

options.text: The maker of the statement must intend it to be relied upon as a contractual warranty, not as a mere representation or opinion
The recipient must in fact have relied on the statement when deciding to enter the main contract
Entering into the main contract can constitute consideration for the collateral promise
The fact that the collateral term contradicts a written term of the main contract is not necessarily fatal to the collateral contract

feedback: Well matched. These four elements encapsulate the law of collateral contracts. Note on inconsistency: *City & Westminster Properties v Mudd* [1959] demonstrates that a collateral contract can flatly contradict a written term. The court will enforce the collateral promise because it is a separate contract supported by its own consideration — the tenant's entry into the lease.
oscoaReferences: Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078

question: BrewCo negotiates to lease a pub from PubCo. The written lease contains a clause requiring BrewCo to purchase all beer exclusively from PubCo at listed prices. Before signing, PubCo's regional manager tells BrewCo's director orally: 'Don't worry about the exclusive supply clause — if you find a better deal elsewhere, you can buy from them. We just put that in as standard but never enforce it.' BrewCo signs the lease, relying on this assurance. Three months later, PubCo enforces the exclusive supply clause. Applying *City & Westminster Properties v Mudd* [1959], what is BrewCo's best argument?

options.text: BrewCo has no defence because the written lease expressly requires exclusive purchasing and parol evidence cannot contradict a written document
options.feedback: Incorrect. The parol evidence rule does not prevent proof of a collateral contract. A collateral contract is a separate agreement and may be proved by oral evidence even if it contradicts the written main contract.

options.text: The regional manager's oral assurance created a collateral contract: it was intended to be relied upon, BrewCo did rely on it by entering the lease, and entry into the lease was consideration — the collateral contract overrides the written exclusive supply clause
options.feedback: Correct. This closely mirrors the facts of *City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd* [1959] Ch 129. The oral assurance induced the lease; the lease entry was consideration; the collateral contract can override the inconsistent written term. The parol evidence rule does not bar proof of a collateral contract.

options.text: BrewCo can only claim for misrepresentation and must prove fraud
options.feedback: Incorrect. BrewCo has a stronger claim in collateral contract which does not require proof of fraud. Collateral contract is a contractual claim; misrepresentation is tortious. The advantage of collateral contract is that damages are contractual (expectation measure), not tortious (reliance measure).

options.text: The oral assurance was a mere representation that cannot be enforced because the written contract contains an entire agreement clause
options.feedback: Incorrect. While there may be an entire agreement clause, collateral contracts are a recognised exception. The existence of an EAC does not prevent the court from finding a separate collateral contract where the tripartite test (intention, reliance, consideration) is satisfied.

feedback: This scenario applies the *Mudd* [1959] principle directly. The key takeaway: a collateral contract can override the express terms of a written agreement. The parol evidence rule does not prevent a party from proving a separate collateral contract — it only prevents contradiction of the terms of the main contract itself. The collateral contract is a distinct agreement with its own consideration (entry into the main contract).
oscoaReferences: City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30

question: Which TWO of the following are required to establish a binding collateral contract under English law?

options.text: The maker of the statement must have intended it to be relied upon as a contractual promise
options.feedback: Correct. *Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton* [1913] AC 30 — Lord Moulton held the statement must be intended as a warranty. A mere statement of opinion or representation will not suffice.

options.text: The collateral statement must be consistent with all terms of the main written contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. *City & Westminster Properties v Mudd* [1959] — the collateral contract directly contradicted a written covenant. Inconsistency is not fatal to a collateral contract.

options.text: The statement must be in writing and signed by both parties
options.feedback: Incorrect. Collateral contracts may be entirely oral. *Mudd* [1959] and *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976] both involved oral assurances. There is no requirement for writing.

options.text: The recipient of the statement must have relied on it when deciding to enter the main contract
options.feedback: Correct. Actual reliance is an essential element. The collateral contract will not arise if the recipient would have entered the main contract regardless of the statement, or did not take it into account.

options.text: Independent consideration beyond entry into the main contract must be provided by the promisee
options.feedback: Incorrect. The entry into the main contract itself can constitute sufficient consideration for the collateral promise (*Mudd* [1959]). No separate monetary payment is required.

feedback: The correct answers are (a) and (d). A collateral contract requires intention that the statement be relied upon as a promise (*Heilbut, Symons v Buckleton* [1913]) and actual reliance by the recipient. Consideration can be provided by entering into the main contract (*Mudd* [1959]), inconsistency with the main contract is not fatal, and the collateral promise need not be in writing.
oscoaReferences: Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129

question: In J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea __________ Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078, the Court of Appeal held that an oral assurance — that goods would be shipped below deck on a new type of container vessel — created a collateral contract that overrode printed exclusion clauses in the main contract.
feedback: Correct. *J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd* [1976] 1 WLR 1078 — a landmark case where an oral collateral contract was held to override standard terms. The defendant's oral assurance induced the claimant to continue using the defendant's shipping services. Lord Denning MR and Roskill LJ emphasised the commercial reality: the oral promise was intended to be binding and was relied upon.
oscoaReferences: J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078

question: CargoCo regularly ships goods for ImportCo under a standard form contract containing wide exclusion clauses and an entire agreement clause. Before the latest shipment, ImportCo's logistics manager asked CargoCo's director: 'These goods are temperature-sensitive — can you guarantee they'll be kept below 4°C?' CargoCo's director replied: 'Absolutely — all your containers will go in our new refrigerated hold. You have my word.' ImportCo placed the order. The goods arrived spoiled after being shipped in an unrefrigerated hold. Applying *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976], what is ImportCo's strongest argument?

options.text: ImportCo has no claim because the written standard terms and EAC exclude all oral statements
options.feedback: Incorrect. The EAC in the main contract does not prevent enforcement of a separate collateral contract. On facts identical to *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976], the oral assurance created a binding obligation.

options.text: ImportCo can claim in negligence only, not contract, because the assurance was not in writing
options.feedback: Incorrect. The claim is in contract — a collateral contract. Contractual damages (expectation measure) may be more generous than tortious damages for negligence.

options.text: The director's oral assurance created a collateral contract which overrides the written exclusion clauses and EAC — ImportCo relied on it when placing the order, and placing the order was consideration
options.feedback: Correct. Following *J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd* [1976] 1 WLR 1078, the oral promise to ship in a refrigerated hold was intended to be binding, was relied upon by ImportCo, and the placement of the order provided consideration. The collateral contract trumps the written exclusion clauses.

options.text: The oral assurance is unenforceable for lack of certainty — 'you have my word' is too vague
options.feedback: Incorrect. The core promise — 'all your containers will go in our new refrigerated hold' — is specific and certain. The phrase 'you have my word' reinforces, rather than undermines, the promissory intent.

feedback: This is a direct application of *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976]. The case demonstrates that in a commercial relationship, oral assurances given to induce a party to contract can override even carefully drafted exclusion clauses and entire agreement clauses. The court looks at the substance: was a promise made, was it intended to be relied upon, and was it relied upon? If yes to all three, a collateral contract is born.
oscoaReferences: J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'A collateral contract can only exist if it is in writing and signed by both parties. It must also be consistent with all terms of the main contract — if it contradicts any written term, it is automatically void. Furthermore, a collateral contract requires independent consideration separate from entry into the main contract, such as a separate payment of money.'
answer: A collateral contract need not be in writing; an oral assurance can suffice. It can directly contradict written terms of the main contract (*Mudd* [1959], *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976]). Entering into the main contract itself can serve as consideration for the collateral promise.
feedback: Found it. Three errors: (1) Collateral contracts can be oral — *City & Westminster v Mudd* [1959] and *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976] both involved oral assurances. (2) A collateral contract can contradict the main contract — that is the whole point of the doctrine; inconsistency is not fatal (*Mudd* [1959]). (3) Entry into the main contract itself constitutes consideration for the collateral promise — no separate payment of money is required (*Heilbut, Symons v Buckleton* [1913]).
oscoaReferences: City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078
Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30

teachingSummary:
**Core Rule:** Parties to a contract may exclude or vary terms that would otherwise be implied by statute or common law. However, such exclusions are subject to strict statutory controls designed to prevent unfair contracting. Under the Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55, implied terms — including satisfactory quality (s.14(2)) and fitness for purpose (s.14(3)) — may be negatived or varied by express agreement, course of dealing, or usage, but any such exclusion in a business-to-business contract is subject to the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 (UCTA) requirement of reasonableness. In consumer contracts, the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs.

**Long-Term Supply Contracts:** In *Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd* [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm), the Commercial Court considered the exclusion of SGA implied terms in a long-term petroleum supply contract between sophisticated commercial parties. The case illustrates how parties may negotiate exclusion clauses tailored to their commercial relationship, but any such clause must still satisfy the UCTA reasonableness test where applicable.

**Interaction with Entire Agreement Clauses:** An EAC alone is generally insufficient to exclude implied statutory terms. EACs target pre-contractual statements and representations, not terms implied by operation of law. Express words are required to exclude implied terms, and the exclusion must comply with the relevant statutory regime (UCTA 1977 for B2B; CRA 2015 for consumers). Additionally, s.3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 subjects any term excluding liability for pre-contractual misrepresentations to the reasonableness requirement, creating a further interaction between EACs and statutory controls.

**In short:** Implied terms can be excluded, but doing so requires express wording and compliance with statutory controls. An entire agreement clause is not a substitute for a properly drafted exclusion clause, and both must satisfy the reasonableness test where applicable.
oscoaReferences: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Consumer Rights Act 2015

question: Under section __________ of the Sale of Goods Act 1979, implied terms — such as the obligation to supply goods of satisfactory quality — may be negatived or varied by express agreement, but any such exclusion in a business contract is subject to the requirement of reasonableness under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.
feedback: Correct. Section 55 of the Sale of Goods Act 1979 permits contracting out of the implied terms in ss.13–15 (correspondence with description, satisfactory quality, fitness for purpose, and sale by sample). However, the ability to exclude these terms is not unfettered — the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977 imposes constraints, requiring that any exclusion or restriction of liability for breach of these implied terms must satisfy the requirement of reasonableness.
oscoaReferences: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question: In __________ Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm), the Commercial Court considered the exclusion of implied terms under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 in the context of a long-term international petroleum supply agreement between sophisticated commercial parties.
feedback: Correct. *Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd* [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm) — this decision illustrates the approach of the Commercial Court to exclusion clauses in sophisticated, long-term supply contracts. The parties had negotiated an exclusion of SGA implied terms, reflecting the bespoke nature of their commercial relationship.
oscoaReferences: Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)

question: Match each statute to its role in regulating the exclusion of contractual terms:
answer: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55=Permits contracting out of implied terms (description, quality, fitness, sample) by express agreement, subject to UCTA 1977; Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977=Subjects exclusion and limitation clauses in business contracts to the requirement of reasonableness; Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3=Subjects any term excluding or restricting liability for pre-contractual misrepresentations to the UCTA reasonableness test; Consumer Rights Act 2015=Regulates exclusion of implied terms and liability in consumer contracts; replaces UCTA and SGA for consumer dealings

options.text: Permits contracting out of implied terms (description, quality, fitness, sample) by express agreement, subject to UCTA 1977
Subjects exclusion and limitation clauses in business contracts to the requirement of reasonableness
Subjects any term excluding or restricting liability for pre-contractual misrepresentations to the UCTA reasonableness test
Regulates exclusion of implied terms and liability in consumer contracts; replaces UCTA and SGA for consumer dealings

feedback: Well matched. These four statutes create a comprehensive statutory framework for the control of exclusion clauses. Note the distinction: UCTA 1977 governs business-to-business contracts; the Consumer Rights Act 2015 governs consumer contracts; s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 specifically addresses pre-contractual misstatements; and SGA 1979, s.55 provides the baseline rule that implied terms are default rules, not mandatory terms.
oscoaReferences: Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Consumer Rights Act 2015

question: PetroRefine Ltd enters a 10-year supply contract with DrillCo Ltd for crude oil. The contract is detailed and negotiated between in-house legal teams of both companies. It states: 'All implied terms, including those under the Sale of Goods Act 1979, are excluded. The buyer accepts the goods “as is” and assumes all risk as to quality.' A delivery of oil has an unusually high sulphur content, causing damage to PetroRefine's refinery. Under the SGA 1979, oil must be of satisfactory quality. PetroRefine sues. Applying UCTA 1977 and *Exxonmobil v Texaco* [2003]:

options.text: The exclusion clause is automatically void because SGA 1979 implied terms can never be excluded in any contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Section 55 of the SGA 1979 expressly permits exclusion of implied terms. The question is not whether they can be excluded, but whether this particular exclusion satisfies the UCTA 1977 reasonableness requirement.

options.text: PetroRefine succeeds automatically because the goods were not of satisfactory quality and the exclusion clause is unfair
options.feedback: Incorrect. The court must first determine whether the exclusion clause, properly construed, covers the breach. Then it must decide whether the clause satisfies the UCTA reasonableness requirement.

options.text: The court will assess whether the exclusion clause satisfies the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test — considering factors including the strength of bargaining positions, whether PetroRefine received an inducement to agree, and whether it knew or ought to have known of the clause
options.feedback: Correct. Under UCTA 1977, s.11 and Schedule 2, the court must assess reasonableness. Factors include: (a) the parties' relative bargaining strength; (b) any inducement received by the buyer; (c) whether the buyer knew or ought reasonably to have known of the clause; (d) whether goods were specially manufactured to the buyer's order; (e) whether compliance with the implied term was practicable at the time of contracting. The fact that both parties are sophisticated commercial entities with in-house counsel militates in favour of reasonableness (*Exxonmobil v Texaco* [2003]), but is not determinative.

options.text: The exclusion clause is valid because all commercial parties are free to contract on any terms they wish without judicial oversight
options.feedback: Incorrect. While party autonomy is respected, UCTA 1977 imposes mandatory controls on exclusion clauses. The court must assess reasonableness — party autonomy is a factor, but it does not exclude the statutory regime.

feedback: UCTA 1977 is the key. The court will assess reasonableness by reference to the guidelines in Schedule 2. The fact that the contract was negotiated between sophisticated parties with legal representation is a factor supporting reasonableness — but it is not conclusive. The breadth of the exclusion ('all implied terms excluded') and the radical risk shift ('as is', 'assumes all risk') will also be considered. *Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd* [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm) illustrates the court's approach to commercial contracts of this type.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.11
Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)
Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55

question: Which TWO of the following correctly state the law on excluding implied terms under English law?

options.text: Implied terms under the Sale of Goods Act 1979 can never be excluded — they are mandatory in all contracts
options.feedback: Incorrect. SGA 1979, s.55 expressly permits exclusion. The implied terms in ss.13–15 are default rules, not mandatory terms. They can be excluded, varied, or restricted, subject to the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test.

options.text: An exclusion of SGA implied terms in a business-to-business contract must satisfy the UCTA 1977 requirement of reasonableness
options.feedback: Correct. UCTA 1977 applies to business contracts. The party relying on the exclusion clause must show it was fair and reasonable to incorporate it, having regard to circumstances known or reasonably contemplated at the time of contracting.

options.text: An entire agreement clause alone is generally insufficient to exclude implied statutory terms — express wording and compliance with statutory controls are required
options.feedback: Correct. EACs address pre-contractual statements and representations. Implied terms arise by operation of law. A general EAC does not have the effect of excluding statutory implied terms — express words to that effect and compliance with UCTA 1977 (or CRA 2015 for consumers) are required.

options.text: Section 3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 has no application to entire agreement clauses because EACs operate only on contractual terms
options.feedback: Incorrect. Section 3 is specifically engaged when an EAC goes beyond declaring the written terms and purports to exclude liability for pre-contractual misrepresentations. *AXA Sun Life* [2011] confirms the interaction.

options.text: The Consumer Rights Act 2015 applies the same reasonableness test as UCTA 1977 to consumer contracts
options.feedback: Incorrect. The CRA 2015 creates a different test — the 'fairness' test — for consumer contracts. UCTA 1977 (reasonableness) governs business contracts; CRA 2015 (fairness) governs consumer contracts. Different statutes, different tests.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (c). UCTA 1977 subjects exclusion clauses in business contracts to the reasonableness requirement. An EAC alone does not exclude implied terms — express wording is needed, and the exclusion must satisfy the statutory test (UCTA 1977 for B2B or CRA 2015 for consumers). The SGA 1979 implied terms are default rules that can be excluded, not mandatory rules.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.55
Consumer Rights Act 2015
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3

question:
Spot the legal error in this paragraph:

'An entire agreement clause is an all-in-one solution. It simultaneously excludes all pre-contractual representations, all collateral warranties, and all implied statutory terms. A well-drafted EAC means a party need never worry about UCTA 1977, the Misrepresentation Act 1967, or the Consumer Rights Act 2015. Once an EAC is in the contract, the parties have complete freedom to define their obligations without any statutory interference.'
answer: An EAC does not automatically exclude implied statutory terms (express wording and compliance with UCTA 1977/CRA 2015 are required). It does not displace the UCTA reasonableness test for excluding liability for misrepresentation (s.3 MA 1967). It does not eliminate the possibility of a collateral contract. Statutory controls — UCTA 1977, MA 1967, CRA 2015 — can override contractual terms that fail the reasonableness or fairness tests.
feedback: Found it. The paragraph overstates the effect of an EAC. While an EAC is a powerful contractual tool, it is subject to statutory controls. (1) It does not automatically exclude implied statutory terms — express wording and compliance with the relevant statutory regime are required. (2) If it purports to exclude liability for misrepresentation, s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 subjects that exclusion to the UCTA reasonableness test. (3) A collateral contract may be found despite an EAC. (4) The courts retain their equitable jurisdiction, including rectification. Statutory controls are mandatory; a clause cannot simply 'contract out' of them.
oscoaReferences: Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Consumer Rights Act 2015
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133

question: EquipCo Ltd sells industrial machinery to ManuFact Ltd. The contract contains an entire agreement clause and states: 'All conditions, warranties, and other terms implied by statute or common law are excluded from this agreement to the fullest extent permitted by law.' The machinery breaks down due to a latent defect that makes it unfit for its purpose. Under SGA 1979, s.14(3), goods must be reasonably fit for their purpose. EquipCo argues the exclusion clause bars any claim. ManuFact argues the clause is unreasonable under UCTA 1977. The contract was signed without negotiation; ManuFact had no legal advice; EquipCo is a much larger company. What is the likely outcome?

options.text: The exclusion clause is valid because it is clearly expressed and contained in a signed contract, and the parol evidence rule prevents challenge
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA 1977 overrides the parol evidence rule and party autonomy. The clause, even if clearly drafted and signed, must additionally satisfy the reasonableness test.

options.text: The clause is likely to be held unreasonable under UCTA 1977 given the inequality of bargaining power, the lack of opportunity for ManuFact to negotiate, and the fact that the defect goes to the core purpose of the machinery
options.feedback: Correct. Applying UCTA 1977, Schedule 2 guidelines: ManuFact had weaker bargaining power, received no inducement to accept the exclusion, had no realistic opportunity to contract elsewhere without similar terms, had no legal advice, and the defect undermined the very purpose for which the machinery was bought (fitness for purpose under s.14(3)). The clause excludes all implied terms — a wholesale exclusion — which is harder to justify as reasonable than a tailored limitation.

options.text: The clause will be upheld because the SGA 1979 permits any exclusion of implied terms without restriction
options.feedback: Incorrect. SGA 1979, s.55 permits exclusion but UCTA 1977 imposes the reasonableness condition on that permission. s.55 is not a free pass; it is the gateway through which UCTA applies.

options.text: The exclusion clause is void because implied terms can never be excluded when goods are supplied to a business
options.feedback: Incorrect. Implied terms can be excluded between businesses — UCTA 1977 contemplates and regulates such exclusions; it does not prohibit them outright. The question is whether this particular exclusion is reasonable.

feedback: This illustrates the practical operation of the UCTA reasonableness test. The Schedule 2 guidelines are not a checklist to be mechanically applied — they are factors for the court to weigh. Here, multiple factors point to unreasonableness: inequality of bargaining power, no opportunity to negotiate, no legal advice, wholesale nature of the exclusion, and the core purpose of the goods being undermined. Compare with *Exxonmobil v Texaco* [2003] where sophisticated parties with equality of arms negotiated a bespoke contract — the reasonableness analysis would likely point the other way.
oscoaReferences: Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977, s.11, Schedule 2
Sale of Goods Act 1979, s.14(3), s.55
Exxonmobil Sales and Supply Corporation v Texaco Ltd [2003] EWHC 1964 (Comm)

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & B] FinCo lends £500,000 to DevCo under a detailed written loan agreement containing an entire agreement clause. Before signing, FinCo's director told DevCo's director orally: 'We won't call in the loan for at least 3 years, whatever the documents say.' The written agreement allows FinCo to demand repayment on 30 days' notice. After 6 months, FinCo demands repayment. DevCo refuses, citing the oral assurance. Applying *Inntrepreneur* [2000] and *City & Westminster v Mudd* [1959], what is the most likely outcome?

options.text: DevCo wins — the oral assurance is a term of the contract despite the EAC
options.feedback: Incorrect. The EAC succeeds in preventing the oral statement from becoming a term of the loan agreement (*Inntrepreneur* [2000]). The issue is whether a separate collateral contract exists.

options.text: DevCo loses — the EAC conclusively bars any claim, and no collateral contract could ever override a written loan agreement
options.feedback: Incorrect. While the EAC prevents the statement from being a written term, it does not preclude a separate collateral contract. The court must examine whether the elements of a collateral contract are met.

options.text: DevCo may succeed on a collateral contract claim if it can prove the oral assurance was intended to be relied upon, was in fact relied upon, and entry into the loan agreement was consideration — but it faces the evidential difficulty of proving an oral promise contradicting a clear written term
options.feedback: Correct. The EAC prevents the oral assurance from being a term of the main contract (*Inntrepreneur* [2000]), but a collateral contract claim is a separate cause of action. DevCo must satisfy the tripartite test — intention, reliance, consideration (*Heilbut, Symons v Buckleton* [1913]; *Mudd* [1959]). The evidential burden is heavy: the court will scrutinise an oral promise that directly contradicts a carefully drafted written agreement. But if DevCo discharges the burden, the collateral contract can override the written terms.

options.text: DevCo wins automatically because any oral promise made before a contract is signed creates a collateral contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. Not every pre-contractual statement creates a collateral contract. The statement must satisfy the three requirements — intention, reliance, and consideration. An offhand remark or vague assurance will not suffice (*Heilbut* [1913]).

feedback: This consolidates Blocks A and B. The EAC succeeds in its primary purpose — the oral statement is not a term of the main contract. But a collateral contract claim remains available. The synergy between the doctrines: an EAC drives a claimant towards the collateral contract route, where they must prove intention, reliance, and consideration. The practical lesson: oral assurances contradicting written terms face an uphill evidential battle but are not automatically defeated.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks A & C] In __________ Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133, the Court of Appeal confirmed that an entire agreement clause does not bar a claim for misrepresentation, but any term excluding misrepresentation liability is subject to the reasonableness requirement under s.3 of the Misrepresentation Act 1967 and UCTA 1977.
feedback: Correct. *AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd* [2011] EWCA Civ 133 is the critical consolidation authority linking EACs (Block A) with statutory controls on exclusion clauses (Block C). The case establishes that an EAC is not a silver bullet — misrepresentation claims survive, subject to the reasonableness filter.
oscoaReferences: AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Blocks B & C] Which TWO of the following correctly describe the interaction between collateral contracts, entire agreement clauses, and statutory controls on exclusion clauses?

options.text: A collateral contract can never override a written term in a contract that also contains an entire agreement clause
options.feedback: Incorrect. A collateral contract is an independent agreement. The presence of an EAC in the main contract does not preclude the enforcement of a collateral contract based on a pre-contractual statement (*Mudd* [1959], *J Evans v Andrea Merzario* [1976]).

options.text: A party may have concurrent claims — a misrepresentation claim (subject to s.3 MA 1967), a collateral contract claim, or both — even where the main contract contains an EAC
options.feedback: Correct. The doctrines are not mutually exclusive. A claimant may argue in the alternative: (1) there was a misrepresentation inducing the contract (subject to s.3 MA 1967); and/or (2) there was a collateral contract. The EAC is relevant but not dispositive of either claim.

options.text: If a contract contains an EAC, UCTA 1977 has no application because the EAC has conclusively determined the parties' entire agreement
options.feedback: Incorrect. UCTA 1977 is a mandatory statutory regime. Contractual terms — including EACs — cannot oust its application. The court must still assess the reasonableness of any clause excluding liability for misrepresentation, implied terms, or negligence.

options.text: Where a contract contains both an EAC and an exclusion of implied terms, the exclusion of implied terms must independently satisfy the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test
options.feedback: Correct. An EAC does not double as an exclusion of implied statutory terms. If the contract separately excludes SGA implied terms, that exclusion must comply with UCTA 1977. The two clauses serve different functions — the EAC addresses pre-contractual statements; the exclusion clause addresses terms imported by law.

options.text: A collateral contract is a type of implied term and is therefore automatically excluded by any exclusion clause in the main contract
options.feedback: Incorrect. A collateral contract is an express, separate contract — not an implied term. It is not automatically excluded by an exclusion clause in the main contract because it is a freestanding agreement with its own legal force.

feedback: Correct answers: (b) and (d). These highlight the layered nature of contractual protection. A well-advised party will consider all potential routes — contractual term (blocked by EAC), collateral contract (separate agreement), misrepresentation (survives EAC, subject to reasonableness), and statutory implied terms (excluded only if reasonable under UCTA). The doctrines complement and constrain each other.
oscoaReferences: AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Cross-Block] PharmCo buys a specialised packaging machine from MachInd Ltd for £2 million. The written contract contains: (i) an entire agreement clause, (ii) a clause excluding all SGA implied terms 'to the extent permitted by law,' and (iii) a clause excluding liability for any pre-contractual statements 'except for fraud.' Before signing, MachInd's engineer told PharmCo's technical director orally that the machine could process 10,000 units per hour. The machine processes only 4,000 units per hour. PharmCo sues. In considering PharmCo's potential causes of action, which of the following is the most comprehensive analysis?

options.text: PharmCo has only one claim — breach of the SGA implied term of fitness for purpose — because the EAC excludes all other routes
options.feedback: Incorrect. The EAC does not extinguish misrepresentation claims or collateral contract claims. PharmCo may have multiple claims. Additionally, the SGA exclusion clause must itself satisfy UCTA 1977.

options.text: PharmCo has no claim because both the EAC and the exclusion clause are clearly drafted and PharmCo signed the contract after the oral statement was made
options.feedback: Incorrect. This gives too much weight to contractual form over substance. Misrepresentation claims survive an EAC; collateral contracts are a separate cause of action; and exclusion clauses must be reasonable under UCTA 1977. Signature is not a blanket waiver of all rights.

options.text: PharmCo has a straightforward claim for breach of contract because the oral statement about processing speed was incorporated as a term by the parties' conduct
options.feedback: Incorrect. The EAC prevents the oral statement from being incorporated as a term of the main written contract (*Inntrepreneur* [2000]). PharmCo must look to other doctrines.

options.text: PharmCo may have up to four potential claims: (1) misrepresentation — the oral statement was a false statement inducing the contract (survives EAC; exclusion of liability subject to s.3 MA 1967 reasonableness); (2) collateral contract — the engineer's assurance may have been intended to be relied upon, was relied upon, and entry into the contract was consideration; (3) breach of SGA implied term — fitness for purpose under s.14(3), but the exclusion clause must satisfy UCTA 1977 reasonableness; (4) breach of SGA implied term — satisfactory quality under s.14(2), subject to same UCTA analysis
options.feedback: Correct. This comprehensive analysis demonstrates the layered protections available to a commercial party. The EAC blocks only the direct contractual route (oral statement as a term). Four other claims remain open: misrepresentation, collateral contract, fitness for purpose, and satisfactory quality — each subject to its own doctrinal and statutory constraints (s.3 MA 1967 for misrepresentation; tripartite test for collateral contract; UCTA 1977 reasonableness for implied terms). A well-advised claimant pleads all available routes in the alternative.

feedback: This consolidation exercise demonstrates the full analytical framework. A single set of facts can give rise to multiple, alternative causes of action. The EAC is a filter — it removes one route (oral statement as a term) but leaves others open. The skill is identifying which routes survive and what hurdles each must clear: s.3 Misrepresentation Act 1967 for misrepresentation claims, the tripartite collateral contract test for collateral contracts, and UCTA 1977 reasonableness for implied terms. Good contract drafting requires attention to all of these, not just the EAC.
oscoaReferences: Inntrepreneur Pub Co v East Crown Ltd [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 611
AXA Sun Life Services plc v Campbell Martin Ltd [2011] EWCA Civ 133
Misrepresentation Act 1967, s.3
Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977
Sale of Goods Act 1979, ss.14(2), 14(3), 55
City & Westminster Properties (1934) Ltd v Mudd [1959] Ch 129
Heilbut, Symons & Co v Buckleton [1913] AC 30

question: [CONSOLIDATION — Block B] The case of J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea __________ Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078 is an example of a collateral contract overriding exclusion clauses and standard terms in a commercial shipping context.
feedback: Correct. *J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd* [1976] 1 WLR 1078 is a leading Court of Appeal authority on the primacy of a collateral contract. It consolidates learning from Block B — the collateral contract is a separate agreement, and it can trump both exclusion clauses and entire agreement clauses in the main contract.
oscoaReferences: J Evans & Son (Portsmouth) Ltd v Andrea Merzario Ltd [1976] 1 WLR 1078
